rating.relevance_remarks: Content covers automata theory well but the topic title suggests complexity theory which is minimally addressed. The sibling topics (DFA, NFA, epsilon transitions) are partially covered.

rating.tier_remarks: Content is appropriate for early undergraduate or high school introduction, not for B.Tech/MSc Sem 5. Too superficial - lacks application-level MCQs, numerical problems, proofs (e.g., pumping lemma proof), and depth expected at this tier. read.md is ~110 lines vs expected 200-250 lines.
suggestions: Expand to 200-250 lines with deeper coverage of pumping lemma, closure properties, and NFA-to-DFA conversion
Add harder MCQs: proving languages non-regular, computing transitive closure, state minimization problems
Include formal proofs: proof of equivalence of DFA and NFA, proof of pumping lemma
Add more formal mathematical notation throughout
Cover complexity theory aspects more substantially or rename topic to focus on automata only
Include example problems with step-by-step solutions

Include section on limitations: what patterns regex CANNOT capture (balanced parentheses, counting)

rating.tier_remarks: Too syntax-focused for Standard tier. Expected formal proofs connecting regex to automata theory, and deeper analysis of application constraints (e.g., regex vs context-free grammars).
